questions: A widely circulated early-2000s beginner tutorial on UNIX and Linux basics is distributed under a Creative Commons license that permits sharing and adaptation but restricts commercial use and requires derivative works to carry the same license. Identify the specific Creative Commons license version that matches these conditions. Then, drawing from the official license description, explain in detail: (1) the freedoms granted to users, (2) all conditions imposed on reuse and redistribution, (3) any statements about revocability of rights, warranties, and additional restrictions, and (4) how this older license version is positioned in comparison to the newer 4.0 versions of Creative Commons licenses.

An early 2000s beginner-friendly UNIX/Linux educational tutorial was released under an older Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike license and later included a notice recommending adoption of the more internationally robust 4.0 versions of Creative Commons licenses. Suppose the tutorial’s author is considering two 4.0 options: (1) the most permissive license that requires only attribution, and (2) the 4.0 successor to the Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike license. Identify these two specific 4.0 licenses and compare them in detail. For each license, explain the freedoms granted, the obligations imposed (including attribution and any additional restrictions), and whether commercial use is permitted. Then, describe several major improvements introduced in the Creative Commons 4.0 license suite overall, such as changes related to international enforceability, treatment of database rights, moral rights and personality/publicity rights, attribution flexibility, and the mechanism for reinstating rights after a violation. Your answer should synthesize information from the official Creative Commons sources for both licenses and the 4.0 suite generally.

An older beginner-friendly UNIX/Linux educational resource states that it uses an earlier Creative Commons license version and recommends upgrading to the newer 4.0 license suite because it is more internationally robust and user-friendly. Focusing on the Creative Commons 4.0 licenses, identify (1) the license that requires Attribution, NonCommercial use, and ShareAlike distribution, and (2) the license that requires only Attribution and permits commercial use without a ShareAlike condition. For each license, provide its official canonical URL, summarize the freedoms it grants, and list its core conditions. In addition, explain at least four major improvements introduced in the 4.0 license suite compared to earlier 3.0 (and prior) versions, particularly in terms of international enforceability, treatment of database rights, handling of moral/publicity rights, attribution flexibility, license violation reinstatement, or clarity around adaptations.

When publishing a UNIX/Linux tutorial for beginners online—especially if it includes command examples, screenshots, and accompanying datasets for practice—many creators choose a Creative Commons license. Identify the most recent major version of the Creative Commons license suite that is recommended for new works, and explain when it was officially published and why it is considered more internationally robust than prior versions. In your answer, compare how this version treats sui generis database rights and license violations (including any cure or reinstatement period) relative to version 3.0. Additionally, describe how the NonCommercial (NC), NoDerivatives (ND), and ShareAlike (SA) elements operate when applied specifically to databases and text/data mining activities under this latest version.

rounds.question: A widely circulated early-2000s beginner tutorial on UNIX and Linux basics is distributed under a Creative Commons license that permits sharing and adaptation but restricts commercial use and requires derivative works to carry the same license. Identify the specific Creative Commons license version that matches these conditions. Then, drawing from the official license description, explain in detail: (1) the freedoms granted to users, (2) all conditions imposed on reuse and redistribution, (3) any statements about revocability of rights, warranties, and additional restrictions, and (4) how this older license version is positioned in comparison to the newer 4.0 versions of Creative Commons licenses.
checklist_width: Correct identification of Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 license
Description of the freedoms to Share (copy and redistribute) and Adapt (remix, transform, build upon)
Statement that freedoms are irrevocable as long as license terms are followed
Explanation of Attribution requirement (credit, link to license, indicate changes)
Explanation of NonCommercial restriction
Explanation of ShareAlike requirement
Mention of prohibition on additional legal or technological restrictions
Reference to lack of warranties and possible limitation by other rights (e.g., privacy, moral rights)
Explanation that version 4.0 licenses are described as more user-friendly and internationally robust, with recommendation to use 4.0 for new works
checklist_depth: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 (BY-NC-SA 2.0) correctly identified as matching share + adapt permissions, noncommercial restriction, and share-alike requirement
Recognition that this is an older pre-4.0 version explicitly compared against the improved 4.0 international versions
rounds.rationale: The question uses contextual clues (early-2000s UNIX tutorial, noncommercial use, mandatory share-alike) to logically narrow the answer to the BY-NC-SA 2.0 license without naming it directly (Deep reasoning). It then requires aggregation of multiple elements from the official license description—freedoms, conditions, revocability, warranty disclaimers, and comparison to version 4.0—ensuring broad information synthesis across the license documentation (Wide scope).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Notice This is an older version of this license. Compared to previous versions, the 4.0 versions of all CC licenses are more user-friendly and more internationally robust . If
rounds.answer_a:
The license is **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0)**. It matches the stated features exactly: users may share and adapt the work, but not for commercial purposes, and derivatives must be distributed under the same license terms [1][2].

Under this license, users are free to **copy and redistribute** the material in any medium or format, and to **remix, transform, and build upon** it [1]. Creative Commons states that the licensor **cannot revoke these freedoms** as long as the license terms are followed [1]. That makes the granted rights durable, though conditional on compliance.

The conditions are fourfold. First, **Attribution**: users must give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and indicate whether changes were made, without implying endorsement [1]. Second, **NonCommercial**: the material cannot be used for commercial purposes [1]. Third, **ShareAlike**: if users adapt the work, they must distribute contributions under the same license [1]. Fourth, **No additional restrictions**: users may not apply legal terms or technological measures that restrict others from doing what the license permits [1].

The official notice also says **no warranties are given**, and warns that other rights, such as publicity, privacy, or moral rights, may still limit use [1]. Creative Commons further labels version 2.0 an **“older license”** and recommends using one of the **4.0 licenses for new works**, indicating that 4.0 is the current, preferred generation even though 2.0 remains valid for works already released under it [1].

## References
[1] Creative Commons. “Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 Generic (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0).” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/

[2] The Linux Documentation Project / tutorial distribution page for the early-2000s UNIX and Linux basics tutorial identifying its license as CC BY-NC-SA.
rounds.answer_b:
The license described is the **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 Generic (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0)** [1]. It was a common choice for open educational materials in the early 2000s.

**(1) Freedoms Granted:** The license explicitly grants users the freedom to copy, distribute, display, and publicly perform the work. It also permits them to create and distribute derivative works (adaptations, remixes, or transformations) based on the original material [1].

**(2) Conditions for Reuse:** To exercise these freedoms, users must adhere to three conditions. **Attribution (BY)** requires giving appropriate credit to the original creator. **NonCommercial (NC)** strictly forbids using the material for purposes that are primarily intended for or directed toward commercial advantage or monetary compensation. **ShareAlike (SA)** mandates that if a user creates a derivative work, they must distribute it under the identical CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 license [1, 2].

**(3) Revocability, Warranties, and Restrictions:** The license is irrevocable; a licensor cannot revoke the granted freedoms as long as the user complies with the terms. The work is provided "as-is" without warranties of any kind, and the licensor disclaims liability for any damages from its use. Furthermore, users may not apply technological or legal measures that would restrict others from exercising their rights under the license [1].

**(4) Comparison to 4.0 Licenses:** The newer 4.0 versions are more user-friendly and globally applicable. Key improvements include clearer definitions (e.g., for "NonCommercial" and "ShareAlike"), a 30-day grace period to correct license violations before rights are terminated, and more practical attribution rules. Unlike the 2.0 license, which was often "ported" to different legal jurisdictions, the 4.0 license is designed as a single, international standard that functions worldwide [3, 4].

## References

[1] Creative Commons. (2004). *Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 Generic (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0) Legal Code*. [creativecommons.org](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/legalcode)

[2] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 Generic Deed*. [creativecommons.org](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/)

[3] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *What's New in 4.0*. CC Wiki. [wiki.creativecommons.org](https://wiki.creativecommons.org/wiki/What's_New_in_4.0)

[4] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Frequently Asked Questions: What are the major differences between the 4.0 licenses and earlier versions?*. [creativecommons.org/faq/#what-are-the-major-differences-between-the-4-0-licenses-and-earlier-versions](https://creativecommons.org/faq/#what-are-the-major-differences-between-the-4-0-licenses-and-earlier-versions)
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the license as Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0), satisfying the DEEP logic requirement. Both also covered the required checklist items: freedoms (share and adapt), irrevocability, attribution, noncommercial restriction, share-alike requirement, prohibition on additional restrictions, warranty disclaimer, reference to other rights, and positioning relative to 4.0.

On WIDTH and completeness, both are strong. However, Agent B provides slightly richer detail in the 4.0 comparison (e.g., 30-day cure period, international unported design vs. ported licenses), and more explicit explanation of commercial intent language. This better fulfills the “detailed explanation” requirement.

In terms of user experience, Agent B’s numbered structure ((1)–(4)) maps exactly to the prompt’s sub-questions, making it more scannable and aligned with the task. It also includes more citations, increasing perceived authority and grounding. Agent A is clear and well-formatted, but its 4.0 comparison is less detailed and slightly less informative.

Since both are factually accurate and complete, and Agent B wins on structure, citation density, and depth of contextual comparison, the result is B_BETTER rather than MUCH_BETTER.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.question: An early 2000s beginner-friendly UNIX/Linux educational tutorial was released under an older Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike license and later included a notice recommending adoption of the more internationally robust 4.0 versions of Creative Commons licenses. Suppose the tutorial’s author is considering two 4.0 options: (1) the most permissive license that requires only attribution, and (2) the 4.0 successor to the Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike license. Identify these two specific 4.0 licenses and compare them in detail. For each license, explain the freedoms granted, the obligations imposed (including attribution and any additional restrictions), and whether commercial use is permitted. Then, describe several major improvements introduced in the Creative Commons 4.0 license suite overall, such as changes related to international enforceability, treatment of database rights, moral rights and personality/publicity rights, attribution flexibility, and the mechanism for reinstating rights after a violation. Your answer should synthesize information from the official Creative Commons sources for both licenses and the 4.0 suite generally.
checklist_width: CC BY 4.0: Allows sharing and adaptation for any purpose, including commercial use
CC BY 4.0: Requires attribution and prohibits additional legal/technological restrictions
CC BY-NC-SA 4.0: Allows sharing and adaptation but prohibits commercial use
CC BY-NC-SA 4.0: Requires attribution and ShareAlike distribution under the same license
4.0 suite improvement: Globally enforceable and no need for porting
4.0 suite improvement: Explicit inclusion of sui generis database rights
4.0 suite improvement: Waiver/clarification of moral, publicity, and personality rights
4.0 suite improvement: Flexible attribution (e.g., link to separate attribution page)
4.0 suite improvement: 30-day automatic reinstatement after curing a license violation
checklist_depth: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) identified as the attribution-only, most permissive 4.0 license
Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0) identified as the 4.0 successor to the older BY-NC-SA license
Explanation that both licenses are part of the Creative Commons 4.0 suite designed to be more internationally robust and user-friendly
rounds.rationale: The question uses the context of an open UNIX/Linux beginner tutorial released under an older BY-NC-SA license to logically narrow the scope to its 4.0 successors (Deep reasoning). It avoids naming specific documents and instead asks for identification of the relevant 4.0 licenses and their features. It then requires broad aggregation of details from multiple official Creative Commons sources: the CC BY 4.0 deed, the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 deed, and the general documentation describing improvements in the 4.0 license suite (Wide coverage).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Creative Commons worked for more than two years to develop the next generation of CC licenses — the version 4.0 CC license suite. The new licenses are more user-friendly and m
rounds.answer_a:
The two relevant 4.0 licenses are **CC BY 4.0** (Attribution 4.0 International) and **CC BY-NC-SA 4.0** (Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International) [1][2].

**CC BY 4.0** is the most permissive of the two: it allows users to **share** and **adapt** the material in any medium or format, including for **commercial** purposes [1]. Its obligations are limited to **attribution**: users must give appropriate credit, link to the license, indicate if changes were made, and avoid implying endorsement. It also prohibits applying legal or technological restrictions that would prevent others from exercising the licensed freedoms [1].

**CC BY-NC-SA 4.0** grants the same basic freedoms to **share** and **adapt**, but only for **noncommercial** purposes [2]. It imposes the same attribution requirements and the same ban on additional legal/technical restrictions. In addition, it includes **ShareAlike**: if someone remixes, transforms, or builds upon the work, they must distribute their contributions under the **same license** [2]. Commercial use is therefore not allowed under this license.

Creative Commons describes the **4.0 suite** as improved over older versions in several major ways. First, 4.0 is drafted to be more **internationally robust and enforceable**, replacing the older ported approach with a single international license intended to work worldwide [3]. Second, 4.0 expressly covers **sui generis database rights**, clarifying reuse of databases across jurisdictions [3]. Third, it more clearly addresses **moral rights** and **publicity/privacy/personality rights** by not licensing them broadly, but requiring licensors to waive or not assert certain rights as needed for licensed use where possible [3]. Fourth, attribution is made more **flexible and practical**, including a more sensible approach for reuse in different media [3]. Finally, 4.0 introduces an important **reinstatement mechanism**: rights automatically return if a violation is cured within 30 days of discovery, rather than terminating permanently for minor mistakes [3].

## References
[1] Creative Commons. “Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0).” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/

[2] Creative Commons. “Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0).” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/

[3] Creative Commons Wiki. “4.0.” https://wiki.creativecommons.org/wiki/4.0
rounds.answer_b:
The two Creative Commons 4.0 licenses are **Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0)** and **Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)**.

The CC BY 4.0 license is the most permissive option. It grants users the freedom to share (copy and redistribute) and adapt (remix, transform, build upon) the material for any purpose, including commercially. The sole obligation is Attribution (BY): users must give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made, without implying endorsement by the licensor [1].

In contrast, the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license grants the same freedoms to share and adapt but imposes three conditions. In addition to Attribution (BY), it includes NonCommercial (NC), which prohibits using the material for purposes "primarily intended for or directed toward commercial advantage or monetary compensation." It also requires ShareAlike (SA), meaning any derivative works must be distributed under the identical CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license [2]. Commercial use is strictly forbidden under this license.

The Creative Commons 4.0 license suite introduced several major improvements over earlier versions. A key change is their global design; instead of being "ported" to different jurisdictions, the 4.0 versions are a single international standard, ensuring more consistent enforceability worldwide. The 4.0 licenses now explicitly cover sui generis database rights, which protect collections of data in some countries separately from copyright. Licensors also agree not to assert moral rights (e.g., the right of integrity) and waive any publicity or privacy rights they hold, preventing these from interfering with reuse [3, 4]. Attribution is more flexible, allowing good-faith compliance by passing on existing credit information. A significant user-friendly feature is the automatic reinstatement of rights: if a user violates the terms, their license is restored if they correct the non-compliance within 30 days of discovering it, whereas rights terminated permanently under older versions [3, 4].

## References

[1] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0)*. creativecommons.org. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/

[2] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)*. creativecommons.org. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/

[3] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *What's New in 4.0*. CC Wiki. Retrieved from https://wiki.creativecommons.org/wiki/What%27s_New_in_4.0

[4] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Frequently Asked Questions: What are the major differences between the 4.0 licenses and earlier versions?*. creativecommons.org. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/faq/#what-are-the-major-differences-between-the-4-0-licenses-and-earlier-versions
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the two licenses (CC BY 4.0 and CC BY-NC-SA 4.0), satisfying the DEEP logic requirement. Both also accurately described freedoms, attribution requirements, commercial use distinctions, and major 4.0 improvements (internationalization/no porting, database rights, moral/publicity rights clarification, flexible attribution, and 30-day reinstatement).

However, on WIDTH/completeness, Agent A explicitly states that CC BY 4.0 prohibits applying additional legal or technological restrictions—an important checklist requirement. Agent B does not mention this restriction for CC BY 4.0 (and does not clearly restate it for CC BY-NC-SA 4.0), which is a required sub-point. Agent A also clearly reiterates the “no additional restrictions” clause for both licenses.

In terms of presentation and readability, both are well-structured, use clear paragraphing, and provide citations. Agent B has slightly richer citation coverage, but Agent A’s formatting is equally clear and slightly more explicit in laying out obligations.

Because both are factually strong but Agent A more fully satisfies the detailed checklist (especially the “no additional restrictions” clause), Agent A provides the better overall user experience. The loss is due to a minor completeness gap (WIDE), not a deep logical error.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

Focusing on the Creative Commons 4.0 licenses, identify (1) the license that requires Attribution, NonCommercial use, and ShareAlike distribution, and (2) the license that requires only Attribution and permits commercial use without a ShareAlike condition. For each license, provide its official canonical URL, summarize the freedoms it grants, and list its core conditions. In addition, explain at least four major improvements introduced in the 4.0 license suite compared to earlier 3.0 (and prior) versions, particularly in terms of international enforceability, treatment of database rights, handling of moral/publicity rights, attribution flexibility, license violation reinstatement, or clarity around adaptations.

rounds.rationale: The question uses the logic of an older UNIX/Linux tutorial recommending an upgrade from an earlier CC license to the 4.0 suite (Deep), without naming the specific source. It requires identifying two specific 4.0 licenses based on their legal conditions and comparing them. It then demands aggregation of dispersed details: canonical URLs and terms from two separate license deeds, plus multiple documented improvements from Creative Commons’ 4.0 overview materials (Wide). This ensures multi-entity retrieval and synthesis.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Creative Commons worked for more than two years to develop the next generation of CC licenses — the version 4.0 CC license suite. The new licenses are more user-friendly and m
rounds.answer_a:
The 4.0 license requiring **Attribution + NonCommercial + ShareAlike** is **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)**: **https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/** [1]. It lets users **share** and **adapt** the material in any medium or format, but **not for commercial purposes** [1]. Its core conditions are: **Attribution** (give appropriate credit, link the license, indicate changes, and avoid implying endorsement), **NonCommercial** (no commercial use), **ShareAlike** (adaptations must be distributed under the same license), and **No additional restrictions** (no legal terms or technological measures that prevent others from exercising the licensed freedoms) [1].

The 4.0 license requiring only **Attribution** is **Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0)**: **https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/** [2]. It also allows users to **share** and **adapt** the material in any medium or format, but unlike CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, it **permits commercial use** and does **not** require ShareAlike [2]. Its conditions are **Attribution** and **No additional restrictions**: credit must be given, the license linked, changes indicated, no endorsement implied, and no extra legal or technical barriers imposed on downstream users [2].

Creative Commons identifies several major improvements in the **4.0 suite** over 3.0 and earlier versions. First, 4.0 was designed as a single **international** license suite, improving cross-border enforceability and replacing the older “ported” approach with language intended to work globally [3]. Second, 4.0 expressly addresses **sui generis database rights**, making permissions clearer for jurisdictions recognizing database protection [3]. Third, it clarifies treatment of **moral rights** and **publicity/privacy/personality rights**: these are generally not licensed away wholesale, but licensors agree not to assert certain rights where necessary to allow the licensed uses [3]. Fourth, 4.0 offers more **flexible attribution**, including a practical way to satisfy credit requirements across media [3]. Fifth, it adds a user-friendly **reinstatement mechanism**: rights automatically recover if a violation is cured within 30 days of discovery [3]. It also improves clarity around what counts as an **adaptation** and how ShareAlike applies [3].

## References
[1] Creative Commons. “Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International.” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/

[2] Creative Commons. “Attribution 4.0 International.” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/

[3] Creative Commons Wiki. “4.0.” https://wiki.creativecommons.org/wiki/4.0
rounds.answer_b:
The two Creative Commons 4.0 licenses are:

1.  **Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)**
    *   **URL:** `https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/` [1]
    *   **Freedoms:** This license allows users to copy, redistribute, remix, and adapt the material.
    *   **Conditions:** It imposes three key restrictions. Users must provide **Attribution (BY)** to the original creator. Use is restricted to **NonCommercial (NC)** purposes, meaning it cannot be for commercial advantage or monetary compensation. Any derivative works must be distributed under the identical license, a condition known as **ShareAlike (SA)** [1, 2].

2.  **Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0)**
    *   **URL:** `https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/` [3]
    *   **Freedoms:** This is the most permissive license, granting the freedom to share and adapt the material for any purpose, including commercially.
    *   **Conditions:** The sole requirement is **Attribution (BY)**. Users must give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made, ensuring they do not imply endorsement by the licensor [3, 4].

The Creative Commons 4.0 license suite introduced significant improvements over earlier versions, such as 3.0. Four major enhancements include:

*   **International Enforceability:** Unlike previous versions that were "ported" to different legal systems, the 4.0 licenses are designed as a single, global standard that functions uniformly worldwide [5].
*   **Database Rights Coverage:** The 4.0 suite explicitly includes *sui generis* database rights, which exist in jurisdictions like the European Union. This ensures that the license terms apply to these rights where they exist, preventing them from unexpectedly restricting reuse [2, 5].
*   **Moral and Publicity Rights:** Licensors agree not to assert their moral rights (like the right to object to certain modifications) to the extent needed to allow reuse. They also waive publicity and privacy rights, clarifying that these cannot be used to block the exercise of licensed permissions [2, 5].
*   **Violation Reinstatement:** If a user violates the terms, their rights are automatically reinstated if they correct the violation within 30 days of discovering it. In prior versions, any breach resulted in permanent termination of rights [5].

## References

[1] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International*. creativecommons.org. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
[2] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0) Legal Code*. creativecommons.org. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/legalcode
[3] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Attribution 4.0 International*. creativecommons.org. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/
[4] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) Legal Code*. creativecommons.org. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/legalcode
[5] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *What's New in 4.0*. CC Wiki. Retrieved from https://wiki.creativecommons.org/wiki/What%27s_New_in_4.0
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both Agent A and Agent B correctly identified (1) CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 as the Attribution + NonCommercial + ShareAlike license and (2) CC BY 4.0 as the Attribution-only license permitting commercial use. Both clearly recognize the Creative Commons 4.0 suite as the improved international successor to 3.0. ✅

Second, Width/Completeness: Both provide canonical URLs, summarize freedoms, and list core conditions. Both explain at least four major 4.0 improvements. However, Agent A covers more of the checklist improvements explicitly: internationalization without porting, database rights, moral/publicity rights clarification, flexible attribution practices, 30-day reinstatement, and clearer adaptation rules. Agent B covers four (internationalization, database rights, moral/publicity rights, reinstatement) but omits explicit discussion of attribution flexibility and clarity around adaptations. Thus, A is more comprehensive.

Third, Presentation & User Experience: Both are well-structured and scannable. Agent B uses slightly cleaner bullet formatting and more granular citations, but Agent A is equally readable and slightly richer in substantive detail. Since both are accurate and well-presented, the decision hinges on completeness.

Conclusion: Agent A provides broader coverage of 4.0 improvements while maintaining clarity and structure. Agent B has no factual failures, but is marginally less comprehensive. Therefore, A_BETTER.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.question: When publishing a UNIX/Linux tutorial for beginners online—especially if it includes command examples, screenshots, and accompanying datasets for practice—many creators choose a Creative Commons license. Identify the most recent major version of the Creative Commons license suite that is recommended for new works, and explain when it was officially published and why it is considered more internationally robust than prior versions. In your answer, compare how this version treats sui generis database rights and license violations (including any cure or reinstatement period) relative to version 3.0. Additionally, describe how the NonCommercial (NC), NoDerivatives (ND), and ShareAlike (SA) elements operate when applied specifically to databases and text/data mining activities under this latest version.

Official publication date: 25 November 2013
Explanation that 4.0 is internationally valid and drafted for global enforceability without porting
Description that 4.0 explicitly licenses sui generis database rights
Explanation that version 3.0 handled sui generis database rights differently (compliance not required when only database rights were implicated or not expressly licensed in unported versions)
Description of the 30-day automatic reinstatement (cure period) for license violations in 4.0
Explanation of how NC applies to databases and commercial text/data mining
Explanation of how ND restricts sharing adapted databases or mined outputs that qualify as adaptations
Explanation of how SA requires adaptations of databases to be licensed under the same or a compatible license
checklist_depth: Creative Commons License Suite Version 4.0 + Proof that it is the latest version recommended and published on 25 November 2013
Comparison to Version 3.0 + Proof of different treatment of sui generis database rights and absence of automatic reinstatement
Application of 4.0 license elements (NC, ND, SA) specifically to databases and text/data mining contexts
rounds.rationale: The question is grounded in Creative Commons 4.0 licensing details but frames them within the practical scenario of publishing a UNIX/Linux tutorial with datasets, ensuring domain relevance. The ‘Deep’ element requires identifying the correct license version based on its internationalization, database-rights treatment, and reinstatement mechanism. The ‘Wide’ element requires aggregating publication history, legal improvements, database-rights differences between 3.0 and 4.0, and specific operational details of NC, ND, and SA for databases and text/data mining—information scattered across multiple official Creative Commons resources.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: 4.0 From Creative Commons (If you are looking for the final policy decisions and versioning notes for 4.0, see the Version 4 page. The set of pages you are currently on are th
rounds.answer_a:
The most recent major Creative Commons license version recommended for new works is **version 4.0**. Creative Commons states that 4.0 licenses are the **most up-to-date licenses recommended for use**, and the 4.0 suite was officially published on **November 25, 2013** [1][2]. It is considered more internationally robust because, unlike the older “ported” model, 4.0 was drafted as a single **international** license suite intended to operate across jurisdictions, with language refined through a long global consultation process to improve enforceability and usability worldwide [2][3].

A major difference from **3.0** is treatment of **sui generis database rights**. Version 3.0 did not comprehensively and uniformly address these rights across the suite, while 4.0 expressly licenses them, giving users clearer permission to share and reuse protected databases in jurisdictions that recognize such rights [2][4]. Creative Commons also explains that 4.0 clarifies how license elements apply when database rights are involved, including extraction and reuse of database contents [4].

Another significant improvement is handling of **license violations**. Under 4.0, rights terminate automatically upon violation, but they are **automatically reinstated** if the violation is cured within **30 days of discovery**; rights can also be reinstated expressly by the licensor [5]. Creative Commons presents this as more user-friendly than 3.0, which lacked this built-in global cure-and-reinstatement mechanism in the same form [2].

For databases and **text/data mining**, the 4.0 elements operate as follows. **NC (NonCommercial)** means extraction, reuse, and mining of the database are allowed only for **noncommercial** purposes [4][6]. **ND (NoDerivatives)** is narrower for databases than many assume: under 4.0, merely producing and sharing an **Adapted Material** restriction does not stop a user from privately extracting or mining data, but sharing a modified database or database contents in a way that creates adapted material is restricted by ND [4][7]. **SA (ShareAlike)** requires that if database rights are used to create and share an adapted database, the resulting database must be distributed under the **same license** [4][8]. Creative Commons specifically notes that 4.0 permits technical acts needed for uses such as text and data mining, subject to the applicable license elements [4].

## References
[1] Creative Commons. “About The Licenses.” https://creativecommons.org/share-your-work/cclicenses/  
[2] Creative Commons Wiki. “4.0.” https://wiki.creativecommons.org/wiki/4.0  
[3] Creative Commons. “Considerations for licensors and licensees.” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/  
[4] Creative Commons. “Frequently Asked Questions – Data, databases, and CC licenses.” https://creativecommons.org/faq/#can-i-apply-a-cc-license-to-my-database  
[5] Creative Commons. “Attribution 4.0 International — Legal Code.” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/legalcode  
[6] Creative Commons. “Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International.” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/  
[7] Creative Commons. “Attribution-NoDerivatives 4.0 International.” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nd/4.0/  
[8] Creative Commons. “Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International.” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/
rounds.answer_b:
The most recent major version of the Creative Commons license suite recommended for new works is **Version 4.0 International**, which was officially published on November 25, 2013 [1]. It is considered more internationally robust because it was designed as a single, global license standard. This approach contrasts sharply with the version 3.0 suite, which was "ported" into dozens of jurisdiction-specific legal codes. These ported versions created linguistic and legal variations that could make cross-border use and enforcement unpredictable. The 4.0 suite eliminates this fragmentation, ensuring the terms are interpreted consistently worldwide [1, 2].

Version 4.0 introduced several key improvements over version 3.0, particularly regarding database rights and license violations. The 4.0 suite explicitly includes and licenses *sui generis* database rights—a separate layer of protection for data collections that exists in jurisdictions like the European Union. Version 3.0 licenses did not address these rights, creating legal uncertainty for users in those regions. For license violations, version 4.0 provides a 30-day "cure period" that allows users to correct non-compliance after they become aware of it, leading to the automatic reinstatement of their rights. Under version 3.0, any violation resulted in the immediate and permanent termination of the license, offering no chance to fix a mistake [1, 2].

For a UNIX/Linux tutorial’s accompanying databases or datasets, the 4.0 license elements have specific implications for activities like text and data mining (TDM):
*   **NonCommercial (NC):** This element restricts the use of the database for purposes primarily intended for commercial advantage or monetary compensation. TDM conducted for commercial research would be prohibited, as the activity requires making copies of the NC-licensed material [3].
*   **NoDerivatives (ND):** This restricts sharing an adapted version of the database. However, the ND condition generally does not prevent the act of TDM itself. The factual outputs of TDM are not considered "adaptations" (derivative works) of the database under the license [3, 4].
*   **ShareAlike (SA):** This requires that if a user creates and shares an adaptation of the database (e.g., a new version with added or restructured data), it must be distributed under the same SA license. This obligation is not typically triggered by TDM, as the process involves extracting information rather than creating and sharing an adapted version of the database itself [3, 4].

## References
[1] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *What's New in 4.0*. CC Wiki. Retrieved from https://wiki.creativecommons.org/wiki/What%27s_New_in_4.0
[2] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Frequently Asked Questions: What are the major differences between the 4.0 licenses and earlier versions?*. creativecommons.org. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/faq/#what-are-the-major-differences-between-the-4-0-licenses-and-earlier-versions
[3] Creative Commons. (2020, September 29). *Creative Commons and Text and Data Mining*. creativecommons.org. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/2020/09/29/creative-commons-and-text-and-data-mining/
[4] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-ND 4.0) Legal Code*. creativecommons.org. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/legalcode
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identify Creative Commons Version 4.0 as the latest recommended suite, published on 25 November 2013, and explain its international robustness (non-ported, globally drafted license). Both accurately describe 4.0’s explicit licensing of sui generis database rights, contrast this with 3.0’s weaker/less explicit treatment, and explain the 30-day automatic reinstatement (cure period) introduced in 4.0. Both also apply NC, ND, and SA to databases and text/data mining with generally accurate nuance.

On Width/Completeness, Agent A is slightly more thorough. It more explicitly explains how 4.0 clarifies database-rights coverage and how license elements apply when only database rights are implicated. Its discussion of ND and technical acts for text/data mining is somewhat more precise and aligned with CC guidance. Agent B is accurate but slightly more compressed and somewhat overstated in saying 3.0 violations resulted in "immediate and permanent" termination with no chance to fix, which oversimplifies the reinstatement landscape under 3.0.

On Presentation & User Experience, Agent A provides clearer structure, stronger scannability (bolding, sectioning), and higher citation density, which better matches a search-engine-style response. It feels more comprehensive and reference-backed.

Since both are factually strong and pass Deep and Wide checks, but Agent A delivers better formatting, nuance, and completeness, Agent A wins on user experience grounds.
